claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

Let me start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'd like to ask about Alex's current situation.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, is he at school, college, sixth form, or doing an approved training course?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in education at the moment. He finished school but he's not studying anywhere right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I need to ask a few more questions about Alex's situation.

**Is Alex currently working, and if so, how many hours per week does he work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at the moment. He doesn't have a job right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Let me ask the next question:

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?** (If he's recently left education, this can allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with the careers service or anything like that. He just left school and hasn't signed up for any of those services.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Just one more question to complete my assessment:

**Is Alex claiming any benefits in his own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not claiming any benefits himself. He doesn't get Universal Credit or anything like that in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me review the situation based on the rules:

- Alex is 17 years old
- He is not currently in approved education or training
- He is not working
- He has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services
- He is not claiming benefits in his own right

According to the Child Benefit rules, you can normally get Child Benefit for a child under 16. For children aged 16-19, you can still qualify **if** they stay in approved education or training.

Additionally, if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, Child Benefit can continue for up to 20 weeks.

Since Alex is 17, not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services, you would not currently be eligible for Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**However**, if Alex were to register with a government-sponsored careers service, you could potentially claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks from the date he registers. Alternatively, if he returns to approved education or training, you would become eligible again while he remains in that education or training (up until he turns 20).

I hope this information is helpful. If Alex's circumstances change, you may wish to reassess your eligibility at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old. Under the rules, Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16, or for those aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training. Alex is not currently in approved education or training. Additionally, while Child Benefit can continue for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, Alex has not registered with either of these services. He is also not working and is not claiming benefits in his own right. As Alex does not meet the criteria for children aged 16 or over (not in approved education/training and not registered with careers service/armed services), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old. Alex has left school and is not in approved education or training, not registered with a careers service or armed services, not working, and not claiming benefits. As Alex does not meet the extended eligibility criteria for children aged 16-19, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
